enrichment.short_description: Colossyan is an AI video generator that turns scripts into presenter-led training videos with lifelike AI avatars, multilingual narration, and built-in e-learning tools.
enrichment.headquarters: Budapest, Hungary (also offices in New York and London)
enrichment.seo_title: Colossyan: AI Avatar Video Generator for Training
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Create AI avatar training videos from text with Colossyan. Multilingual dubbing, SCORM export, quizzes, and branching scenarios for L&D teams.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Colossyan, marketed as Colossyan Creator, is an AI video generation platform that converts written scripts and documents into presenter-led videos using photorealistic AI avatars, removing the need for cameras, actors, or studio production. It was founded in 2020 in Budapest, Hungary, by CEO Dominik Mate Kovacs after his frustration with the cost and slowness of traditional corporate video production. The company now operates from offices in Budapest, New York, and London with roughly 100 employees. As of early 2025, Colossyan reported around 35,000 business accounts and close to one million videos generated cumulatively, positioning it primarily as a tool for corporate learning and development (L&D), internal communications, and customer education rather than consumer or entertainment video. The core workflow lets a user type or paste a script, choose from a library of more than 160 AI avatars and 170+ voices (filterable by accent, tone, age, and emotion), and generate a narrated video with natural facial expressions, lip-sync, and gestures matched to the script. Avatar narration, on-screen text, and interactive elements can be localized together in a single workflow, with narration and translation available across dozens of languages depending on the avatar tier, and voice cloning supported in around 29 languages from a one-to-five-minute voice sample. What differentiates Colossyan from general-purpose AI video tools such as Synthesia or HeyGen is its emphasis on e-learning-specific functionality: built-in interactive quizzes, branching decision-tree scenarios, a Brand Kit for consistent logos/fonts/colors, and native SCORM export so finished videos can be uploaded directly into a corporate LMS and track completion and quiz scores. The product also includes AI image generation for slide backgrounds, a built-in screen recorder, and a document-to-video feature that can turn an existing PowerPoint deck or PDF into a video draft in under a minute. Colossyan uses a freemium pricing model. The Starter plan is free and includes a limited monthly allotment of avatar-narration minutes, a smaller avatar and voice library, a handful of interactive videos per month, and unlimited course creation, but does not include SCORM export. The Professional plan is $59/month on annual billing (roughly $30/month per additional editor seat) and adds more avatar minutes across the standard and premium (NEO2) avatar tiers, watermark-free MP4 downloads, SCORM export, AI image generation, and up to three editors. Enterprise pricing is quote-based and unlocks unlimited video minutes, unlimited SCORM exports, custom or branded studio avatars, SSO/SAML, data residency controls, and a dedicated customer success manager. An API is available for programmatic, large-scale video generation with the same feature set as the visual editor, including personalization variables for inserting viewer-specific data, and it connects to more than 5,000 apps via Zapier, though Colossyan lacks deep native integrations with CRM, help desk, or chat platforms beyond that. Typical use cases include compliance and safety training, employee onboarding, sales enablement content, and localized training for global workforces, since a single script can be re-rendered into many languages without re-filming. Reviewers commonly note the avatars can still look synthetic under close scrutiny and that per-minute usage caps on lower tiers can make high-volume use costly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Colossyan is an AI video generator focused on corporate training and learning content. Instead of filming with a camera and a human presenter, users write or paste a script and Colossyan renders a video with an AI avatar that speaks the script with synchronized lip movement, expressions, and gestures.

Founded in 2020 in Budapest by CEO Dominik Mate Kovacs, the company now has offices in Budapest, New York, and London and serves tens of thousands of business accounts, primarily learning and development, HR, and internal communications teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform includes over 160 AI avatars and 170+ voices, custom voice cloning, a Brand Kit for consistent visual branding, and a built-in screen recorder for mixing live screen capture with avatar narration.

E-learning-specific tools set Colossyan apart from general AI video generators: interactive quizzes, branching scenarios, and SCORM export so completed videos can plug directly into a company's LMS and report learner progress and scores.

A document-to-video feature converts existing PowerPoint files, PDFs, or Word documents into a video draft automatically, and an API with Zapier connectivity lets teams trigger video generation from CRM updates, form submissions, or other workflow events.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Colossyan follows a freemium model. The Starter plan is free and covers a limited number of avatar-narration minutes per month with no SCORM export. The Professional plan is $59/month billed annually and adds more avatar minutes, watermark-free downloads, SCORM export, AI image generation, and up to three editor seats.

Enterprise pricing is custom and adds unlimited video minutes, unlimited SCORM exports, branded or custom studio avatars, SSO/SAML, data residency controls, and a dedicated customer success manager for larger, security-conscious organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Multilingual and Localization Capabilities
paragraphs: Colossyan is built around localizing a single piece of training content into many languages at once: avatar narration, on-screen text, and interactive elements can all be translated and dubbed together within the same editor, and voice cloning supports roughly 29 languages.

This makes it a common choice for multinational companies that need to roll out the same compliance, safety, or onboarding training across regional offices without re-filming or hiring local voice talent for each language.

features.title: AI Avatar Video Generation
features.description: Convert scripts into narrated videos using 160+ realistic AI avatars with natural lip-sync, facial expressions, and gestures.

features.title: Multilingual Narration & Dubbing
features.description: Translate and dub avatar narration, on-screen text, and interactive elements together, with voice cloning available in roughly 29 languages.

features.title: Interactive Learning Elements
features.description: Add branching decision-tree scenarios and multiple-choice quizzes directly inside videos to boost learner engagement and check comprehension.

features.title: SCORM Export for LMS
features.description: Export finished videos as SCORM packages that report completion and quiz scores inside most learning management systems.

features.title: Document & Slide Import
features.description: Turn existing PowerPoint decks, PDFs, or Word documents into a video draft with avatar narration automatically.

features.title: Brand Kit & Screen Recorder
features.description: Apply custom logos, fonts, and colors across videos and templates, and record and embed screen captures inside the editor.

features.title: API & Zapier Automation
features.description: Generate videos programmatically with personalization variables, or trigger generation from 5,000+ apps via Zapier.

pros: Genuine free tier for individuals exploring AI video before paying
No filming, actors, or studio production required to update training content
Strong e-learning feature set (SCORM export, quizzes, branching scenarios) that many general AI video tools lack
Fast localization of a single script into many languages without re-shooting
cons: AI avatars can still look synthetic under close inspection, especially in premium/emotive delivery
SCORM export and watermark removal are locked behind paid plans
Limited native integrations outside of Zapier and the API
Minute-based usage caps on lower tiers can get expensive for high-volume video production

faqs.question: Is Colossyan free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Starter plan is free and includes a limited number of avatar-narration minutes per month, though SCORM export and watermark removal require a paid plan.

faqs.question: Does Colossyan support multiple languages?
faqs.answer: Yes, avatar narration, on-screen text, and interactive elements can be translated and dubbed together, with voice cloning supported in around 29 languages.

faqs.question: Can Colossyan videos be uploaded to a learning management system?
faqs.answer: Yes, Professional and Enterprise plans can export finished videos as SCORM packages compatible with most LMS platforms.

faqs.question: Who is Colossyan built for?
faqs.answer: It is aimed primarily at corporate learning and development, HR, and internal communications teams rather than consumer video creators.

faqs.question: Does Colossyan have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, an API is available for programmatic, large-scale video generation with the same feature set as the visual editor, and it also connects to thousands of apps via Zapier.

use_cases.title: Compliance and safety training
use_cases.description: Convert policy documents into narrated training videos with quizzes to track employee completion.

use_cases.title: Multilingual onboarding
use_cases.description: Localize a single onboarding script into many languages without re-filming for each region.

use_cases.title: Sales enablement content
use_cases.description: Turn product one-pagers or slide decks into short avatar-led explainer videos for reps and prospects.

features: Limited AI avatar-narration minutes per month
More avatar minutes across standard and NEO2 avatar tiers
Unlimited standard avatar minutes, custom NEO2 minutes

enrichment.short_description: Comet is Perplexity AI's agentic, Chromium-based web browser with a built-in AI assistant that can answer questions, summarize pages, and complete multi-step tasks.
enrichment.seo_title: Comet by Perplexity: Free AI Agentic Web Browser
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Comet is Perplexity's free AI browser with a built-in agent that browses, researches, and completes tasks for you on Windows, Mac, iOS, and Android.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Comet is an AI-native, Chromium-based web browser built by Perplexity AI, the answer-engine company founded in August 2022 by Aravind Srinivas, Denis Yarats, Johnny Ho, and Andy Konwinski, headquartered in San Francisco with roughly 350 employees and a valuation reported around $20 billion as of late 2025. Comet embeds an AI agent directly into the browser's sidebar rather than as a separate chat window: it knows which tab a user is on, retains context across a browsing session, and can take multi-step actions on a user's behalf, such as summarizing a page, comparing product reviews, drafting an email, or completing a multi-step research task across several tabs. Perplexity first released Comet on July 9, 2025, initially to a limited group of Perplexity Max subscribers paying $200/month, then made it free for everyone worldwide on Windows and macOS starting October 2, 2025. The company subsequently expanded platform coverage, launching Comet on Android in November 2025 and on iOS/iPadOS in March 2026, making it available across desktop and mobile. Perplexity has continued to invest heavily in the product, reportedly raising around $200 million specifically to grow Comet, which the company frames as the "front door" to an emerging agent-driven internet. Core capabilities include Perplexity's cited-answer search built into every page, a background assistant (for Max subscribers) that can handle multi-step tasks like booking or emailing without constant supervision, a native ad blocker, voice mode for hands-free queries on mobile, and, for Max subscribers, access to top-tier models including Claude Opus for more advanced agentic tasks. Perplexity also sells a standalone $5/month add-on called Comet Plus, which bundles AI-enhanced access to publisher news content, positioned as an alternative to services like Apple News. Because Comet is free at its core, its main competitors are other browsers rather than paid SaaS tools: Google Chrome, Microsoft Edge with Copilot, The Browser Company's Dia and Arc, and Opera's Neon are the closest comparisons, with Comet's differentiation being how deeply the AI agent is woven into everyday browsing rather than bolted on as an extension. Comet's agentic capabilities have also drawn significant security scrutiny. Independent researchers at Brave, LayerX, and Zenity Labs documented indirect prompt-injection attack vectors in 2025 and 2026 — including a technique nicknamed "CometJacking," where a single malicious link or a booby-trapped calendar invite could hijack the browser agent's session, and in one demonstrated case, steer it into an authenticated password manager to reveal or change stored credentials, or exfiltrate local files with no further user interaction. Perplexity has published its own security and privacy FAQ addressing some of these concerns and continues to patch reported vulnerabilities, but reviewers and security researchers generally caution that granting an AI agent broad access to logged-in sessions, email, and calendars introduces a materially different risk profile than traditional browsing, and that agentic browsers as a category are still maturing their security architecture.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Comet is Perplexity AI's web browser, built on Chromium, with an AI agent embedded natively in the sidebar rather than offered as a separate extension. The agent is aware of the page a user is viewing, can answer questions about it, and can take actions like summarizing content, comparing options across tabs, or completing simple multi-step tasks.

Perplexity, the company behind Comet, was founded in 2022 by Aravind Srinivas, Denis Yarats, Johnny Ho, and Andy Konwinski and is headquartered in San Francisco. Comet launched in mid-2025 as a paid, invite-limited product before becoming free worldwide later that year.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Comet integrates Perplexity's cited-answer search engine directly into browsing, so users can ask questions about a page or the web generally without switching tools. A sidebar assistant retains context across tabs and can be asked to summarize, compare, or research across multiple open pages.

For subscribers on Perplexity's Max plan, Comet includes a background assistant capable of running multi-step tasks (such as research or booking workflows) with less step-by-step supervision, along with access to more capable underlying models for agentic tasks.
The browser also includes a built-in ad blocker and voice mode on mobile, and is available across Windows, macOS, Android, and iOS as of 2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Comet itself is free to download and use on all supported platforms. Perplexity monetizes the surrounding ecosystem rather than the browser directly: a $5/month Comet Plus add-on provides AI-enhanced access to publisher news content, and Perplexity's $200/month Max subscription unlocks the most advanced agentic features and top-tier model access inside Comet.
There is no separate business or team pricing tier specific to Comet at this time; it is bundled with Perplexity's consumer subscription plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Security and Privacy Considerations
paragraphs: Because Comet's agent can act on a user's behalf inside authenticated sessions (email, calendars, password managers, and other logged-in services), security researchers have identified prompt-injection vulnerabilities that could let a malicious webpage, link, or calendar invite manipulate the agent into leaking data or taking unintended actions.

Perplexity has responded with patches and a published security FAQ, but independent security firms continue to flag agentic browsing as an emerging risk category, and users handling sensitive accounts should weigh convenience against the broader access an AI browser agent requires.

features.title: Native AI Sidebar Assistant
features.description: An AI agent lives in the browser sidebar, aware of the current tab and able to answer questions, summarize, or take action without switching apps.

features.title: Cited Answer Engine Built In
features.description: Perplexity's search and cited-answer capabilities are available on every page, not just a separate search tab.

features.title: Cross-Tab Research
features.description: The assistant retains context across multiple open tabs to help with comparison shopping, research, and multi-step tasks.

features.title: Background Agent (Max)
features.description: Max subscribers get a background assistant that can handle multi-step tasks like booking or emailing with reduced manual supervision.

features.title: Built-In Ad Blocker
features.description: Comet includes native ad blocking without requiring a separate extension.

features.title: Cross-Platform Availability
features.description: Available free on Windows, macOS, Android, and iOS, with voice mode on mobile.

pros: Free to use on all major desktop and mobile platforms
AI assistant is deeply integrated rather than a bolt-on extension
Backed by Perplexity's well-regarded cited-answer search engine
Rapid platform expansion (Android and iOS added within a year of launch)
cons: Independent researchers have found prompt-injection vulnerabilities that can hijack the agent's actions
Granting the agent access to email, calendar, and password manager sessions raises real data-exposure risk
Most advanced agentic features are reserved for the $200/month Max plan
As a newer Chromium-based browser, extension and enterprise-management ecosystem is less mature than Chrome or Edge

faqs.question: Is Comet free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Comet is free to download and use on Windows, macOS, Android, and iOS. Perplexity Max ($200/month) unlocks more advanced agentic features and top-tier model access inside the browser.

faqs.question: Is Comet the same as Comet ML?
faqs.answer: No. Comet here refers to Perplexity AI's agentic web browser. Comet ML is a separate, unrelated company that makes machine learning experiment-tracking software.

faqs.question: What platforms is Comet available on?
faqs.answer: Comet launched on Windows and macOS in 2025, followed by Android in November 2025 and iOS/iPadOS in March 2026.

faqs.question: Is Comet safe to use?
faqs.answer: Perplexity has published security documentation and patched several issues, but independent researchers have demonstrated prompt-injection attacks against Comet's agent, so users should be cautious about granting it access to sensitive accounts.

faqs.question: What is Comet Plus?
faqs.answer: Comet Plus is a separate $5/month add-on that provides AI-enhanced access to publisher news content, distinct from the free Comet browser itself.

use_cases.title: AI-assisted web research
use_cases.description: Ask questions about the current page or research a topic across multiple open tabs without switching to a separate search tool.

use_cases.title: Everyday task automation
use_cases.description: Have the assistant summarize long articles, draft email replies, or compare options across several tabs while browsing.

use_cases.title: Agentic multi-step workflows
use_cases.description: Max subscribers can delegate multi-step tasks like research compilation or booking to the background assistant.

features: Available on Windows, macOS, Android, iOS
AI-enhanced access to publisher news content
Add-on to the free Comet browser
Access to top-tier models for agent tasks
Priority access to new Comet features

enrichment.short_description: CommaFeed is a free, open-source RSS feed reader inspired by Google Reader, available as a free hosted instance or a self-hostable Java/React application.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CommaFeed is a free, open-source Google Reader-style RSS reader. Self-host it with Docker or use the free hosted instance at commafeed.com.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CommaFeed is a free and open-source RSS feed reader modeled on the interface and organizational style of Google Reader, the popular feed reader Google discontinued in 2013. CommaFeed was first publicly released in May 2013, shortly after Google announced Google Reader's shutdown, filling the gap left for users who wanted a similar folder-and-subscription-based reading experience. The project is maintained on GitHub under the account "Athou" and has accumulated more than 3,600 stars and over 400 forks, indicating an active, established open-source community. Technically, CommaFeed's backend is written in Java on the Quarkus framework, chosen for fast startup time and low memory usage, while the frontend is built with React and TypeScript. It supports multiple database backends, including H2 for simple deployments and PostgreSQL, MySQL, or MariaDB for larger installations. The entire codebase is released under the Apache-2.0 license, meaning anyone can inspect, modify, self-host, or fork it without licensing fees. There are two primary ways to use CommaFeed. The first is the free public hosted instance at commafeed.com, which the project states carries no ads, no tracking, and does not sell or exploit user data — effectively a ready-to-use account for anyone who doesn't want to run their own server. The second is self-hosting, which is straightforward via Docker images published to Docker Hub, letting privacy-focused users, homelab enthusiasts, or organizations run their own private instance with full control over their data and infrastructure. Feature-wise, CommaFeed supports OPML import and export for moving subscriptions in and out of other readers, a REST API with Fever-API compatibility (so it can work with some third-party RSS client apps built for the discontinued Fever service), keyboard shortcuts for nearly all actions, four different layout options, light and dark themes, full mobile responsiveness, push notifications for new articles, support for right-to-left languages, a UI translated into more than 25 languages by community contributors, and the ability to apply custom CSS and JavaScript for deep personalization. Because it is a niche, community-maintained open-source tool rather than a funded startup, CommaFeed does not have a formal company, headquarters, or disclosed employee count, and it competes primarily with other self-hostable or privacy-oriented feed readers such as FreshRSS, Tiny Tiny RSS (TT-RSS), and Miniflux, as well as commercial hosted readers like Feedly and Inoreader. Its appeal is concentrated among developers, self-hosting enthusiasts, and users who specifically want a lightweight, ad-free, Google-Reader-like reading experience without vendor lock-in or a subscription fee, rather than users looking for AI summarization or heavy social/discovery features found in some modern feed readers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CommaFeed is an open-source RSS/Atom feed reader designed to recreate the folder-based, keyboard-friendly reading experience of the discontinued Google Reader. It launched in May 2013 in direct response to Google Reader's shutdown announcement.
The project is free, ad-free, and available either through a hosted public instance at commafeed.com or as self-hosted software that anyone can run on their own server or homelab.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: CommaFeed supports OPML import/export for migrating subscriptions, a REST API with Fever-API compatibility for third-party clients, keyboard shortcuts for nearly every action, and four selectable layouts with light and dark themes.

The interface is fully responsive on mobile and desktop, supports push notifications for new articles, right-to-left language feeds, and a UI translated into more than 25 languages, with custom CSS/JavaScript support for advanced personalization.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: CommaFeed is entirely free. The public hosted instance at commafeed.com has no ads or tracking, and the self-hosted version, distributed via Docker images, is free to deploy on your own infrastructure under the Apache-2.0 open-source license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Self-Hosting and Technology
paragraphs: The backend is built with Java on the Quarkus framework for fast startup and low memory footprint, while the frontend uses React and TypeScript. It supports H2 for lightweight setups and PostgreSQL, MySQL, or MariaDB for larger self-hosted deployments.

Self-hosting is the standard route for users who want full control over their reading data and infrastructure, and Docker images are published and updated regularly on Docker Hub to simplify deployment.

features.title: Google Reader-Style Interface
features.description: Folder-based subscription management, unread counts, and keyboard shortcuts recreate the classic Google Reader workflow.

features.title: Self-Hosting via Docker
features.description: Deploy your own private instance with Docker, backed by H2, PostgreSQL, MySQL, or MariaDB.

features.title: Free Hosted Public Instance
features.description: Use the ad-free, tracking-free public instance at commafeed.com without running your own server.

features.title: OPML Import/Export
features.description: Move subscriptions in and out of CommaFeed easily when switching from or to other feed readers.

features.title: REST API with Fever Compatibility
features.description: A documented REST API, including Fever-API compatibility, lets third-party RSS client apps connect to CommaFeed.

features.title: Multi-User Support
features.description: Multiple user accounts let each person curate and follow their own independent list of feeds.

features.title: Deep Customization
features.description: Custom CSS and JavaScript, four layout options, light/dark themes, and a UI translated into 25+ languages.

pros: Completely free, whether self-hosted or using the public instance
Open-source (Apache-2.0) with an active GitHub community and regular releases
No ads or tracking on the official hosted instance
Lightweight Java/Quarkus backend with fast startup and low resource usage for self-hosting
cons: No AI summarization or content-discovery features found in some modern readers
Interface is intentionally minimal and dated compared to commercial readers like Feedly or Inoreader
Self-hosting requires basic server/Docker familiarity to get full control over your data
Maintained largely by a small open-source community rather than a funded company

faqs.question: Is CommaFeed really free?
faqs.answer: Yes. CommaFeed is free and open-source under the Apache-2.0 license, both as a self-hosted deployment and via the free public instance at commafeed.com.

faqs.question: Can I self-host CommaFeed?
faqs.answer: Yes, CommaFeed is designed to be self-hosted, with official Docker images and support for H2, PostgreSQL, MySQL, and MariaDB databases.

faqs.question: Does CommaFeed have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: CommaFeed does not have dedicated native mobile apps, but its web interface is fully responsive and works well on mobile browsers, and it supports Fever-API-compatible third-party RSS apps.

faqs.question: How is CommaFeed different from Feedly or Inoreader?
faqs.answer: CommaFeed is free and open-source with no ad-supported or premium tiers, aimed at users who want a lightweight, self-hostable, Google-Reader-style reader rather than a commercial platform with AI features and paid plans.

faqs.question: Is CommaFeed still maintained?
faqs.answer: Yes, CommaFeed remains an actively maintained open-source project on GitHub with regular releases.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused feed reading
use_cases.description: Self-host CommaFeed to keep full control of reading data instead of relying on a third-party commercial reader.

use_cases.title: Team or organizational news monitoring
use_cases.description: Run a shared self-hosted instance with multiple user accounts so different team members can track their own feed subscriptions.

use_cases.title: Lightweight Google Reader replacement
use_cases.description: Use the free hosted instance for a familiar, ad-free, keyboard-driven RSS reading experience.

features: Free public hosted instance at commafeed.com

enrichment.short_description: Common Room is an AI-native customer intelligence platform that unifies buying signals from 30+ sources to help sales and marketing teams find and act on warm opportunities.
enrichment.seo_title: Common Room: AI Customer Intelligence Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Common Room unifies buying signals from 30+ sources into one GTM platform with AI research agents. Zoom announced a deal to acquire it in July 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Common Room is an AI-native customer intelligence and go-to-market (GTM) platform founded in 2020 in Seattle, Washington, by Francis Luu, Linda Lian, Tom Kleinpeter, and Viraj Mody. The company has raised a total of roughly $104.9 million across funding rounds, including a Series B extension, and has grown to approximately 180 employees. In July 2026, Zoom announced a definitive agreement to acquire Common Room, describing the deal as bringing upstream buyer intelligence into Zoom's AI revenue platform and Zoom Revenue Accelerator product; financial terms were not disclosed, and the transaction was expected to close within weeks of the announcement, though as of the acquisition announcement Common Room continued operating as a distinct product. Common Room's core product captures buying and engagement signals from more than 30 data sources — including Slack, Discord, GitHub, Reddit, LinkedIn, Twitter/X, Stack Overflow, DEV, and Segment — and uses identity resolution to connect that activity to real people and accounts, giving sales, marketing, product, and community teams a unified view of who is engaging with a company across channels. The platform syncs enriched contact and signal data into Salesforce and HubSpot so reps can act on it inside their existing CRM workflow, and it includes AI agent features branded RoomieAI (for AI-assisted research and account summarization) and Prospector (for account and contact discovery), alongside unlimited configurable alerts, workflows, and audience segments. Common Room is priced as an enterprise SaaS product with three public tiers. The Essential plan costs $2,500/month billed annually and includes 5 seats, up to 100,000 tracked contacts, 5,000 monthly RoomieAI research credits, and 2,500 monthly Prospector credits, along with a shared customer success manager. The Advanced plan (15 seats, up to 250,000 contacts, 7,500 RoomieAI and Prospector credits each, dedicated CSM) and Enterprise plan (30 seats, up to 750,000 contacts, 10,000 RoomieAI credits, 15,000 Prospector credits, comprehensive integrations, dedicated CSM) are both custom-quoted. There is no free tier; prospective customers must engage with sales for anything beyond Essential. Common Room is positioned for B2B SaaS companies, particularly those with a developer, open-source, or community-driven go-to-market motion, where signals from GitHub stars, Slack community activity, or product usage can meaningfully predict sales opportunities before a lead ever fills out a form. It competes most directly with Commsor (a separate, unrelated company despite operating in an adjacent category), as well as with community and intent-data platforms like Orbit, Warmly, Endgame, and Clearbit/Breeze-style intent tools. The pending Zoom acquisition signals a broader industry trend of established communication and revenue platforms acquiring specialized buyer-intelligence startups to build end-to-end AI-driven revenue workflows rather than leaving customers to stitch together point solutions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Common Room is a customer intelligence platform that pulls together buying signals from dozens of sources — community platforms, developer tools, social channels, and CRM data — into a single, person-level view for go-to-market teams. It was founded in 2020 in Seattle by Francis Luu, Linda Lian, Tom Kleinpeter, and Viraj Mody.
In July 2026, Zoom announced a definitive agreement to acquire Common Room to add upstream buyer intelligence to its AI revenue platform, a deal expected to close within weeks of the announcement.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform connects to 30+ sources including Slack, Discord, GitHub, Reddit, LinkedIn, Twitter/X, Stack Overflow, and DEV, using identity resolution to tie scattered activity back to real accounts and people.

AI agent features — RoomieAI for research and Prospector for account/contact discovery — automate much of the manual digging sales and marketing teams previously did manually, and unlimited alerts, workflows, and segments let teams act on signals as they happen.
Enriched signal and contact data syncs into Salesforce and HubSpot so reps can act inside the CRM tools they already use.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Common Room publishes one self-serve-adjacent tier, Essential, at $2,500/month billed annually for 5 seats and up to 100,000 contacts. Advanced and Enterprise tiers scale seats, contacts, and AI credit allotments and require a custom quote from sales.
There is no free tier, positioning Common Room as a mid-market-to-enterprise tool rather than a self-serve product for small teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Zoom Acquisition
paragraphs: In July 2026, Zoom entered into a definitive agreement to acquire Common Room, folding its buyer-intelligence capabilities into Zoom's broader AI revenue platform. Financial terms were not disclosed, and the deal was expected to close within weeks, subject to customary closing conditions.

features.title: 30+ Data Source Integrations
features.description: Capture signals from Slack, Discord, GitHub, Reddit, LinkedIn, Twitter/X, Stack Overflow, DEV, Segment, and more in one place.

features.title: Identity Resolution
features.description: Connect scattered activity across channels back to real people and accounts for a unified engagement view.

features.title: RoomieAI Research Agent
features.description: An AI agent that researches accounts and contacts and produces summarized, actionable insights for GTM teams.

features.title: Prospector
features.description: AI-assisted account and contact discovery to find new prospects that match existing buying-signal patterns.

features.title: CRM Sync
features.description: Push enriched contact and signal data into Salesforce and HubSpot so reps can act without leaving their CRM.

features.title: Unlimited Alerts, Workflows & Segments
features.description: Configure real-time alerts and automated workflows triggered by specific buying signals, with no limit on how many are set up.

pros: Very broad set of native data source integrations (30+) compared to most GTM intelligence tools
AI agent features (RoomieAI, Prospector) reduce manual research time for sales and marketing teams
Strong fit for developer- and community-led GTM motions where GitHub/Slack/Discord activity predicts buying intent
Backed by significant funding ($104.9M+) and a pending Zoom acquisition suggesting continued investment
cons: No free tier; entry pricing starts at $2,500/month billed annually
Advanced and Enterprise pricing is not public and requires a sales conversation
Pending Zoom acquisition creates some uncertainty about the product's long-term roadmap and standalone positioning
Best suited to companies with an active community or developer presence; less value for traditional sales-only motions

faqs.question: Is Common Room being acquired?
faqs.answer: Yes. Zoom announced a definitive agreement to acquire Common Room in July 2026 to add buyer intelligence to its AI revenue platform. Financial terms were not disclosed, and the deal was expected to close within weeks of the announcement.

faqs.question: Is Common Room the same company as Commsor?
faqs.answer: No, they are separate companies. Common Room and Commsor operate in adjacent categories (customer intelligence and community/GTM signals, respectively) and have historically been listed as competitors or alternatives, but there has been no merger between them.

faqs.question: What integrations does Common Room support?
faqs.answer: Common Room connects to more than 30 sources including Slack, Discord, GitHub, Reddit, LinkedIn, Twitter/X, Stack Overflow, DEV, Segment, Salesforce, and HubSpot.

faqs.question: Does Common Room have a free plan?
faqs.answer: No, Common Room does not offer a free tier. The lowest published tier, Essential, starts at $2,500/month billed annually.

faqs.question: Who uses Common Room?
faqs.answer: It's primarily used by go-to-market, sales, marketing, community, and product teams at B2B SaaS companies, especially those with developer- or community-driven growth motions.

use_cases.description: Use Prospector and RoomieAI to identify and research new accounts that match existing high-intent signal patterns.

enrichment.short_description: Commsor is a B2B go-to-market platform that maps sales teams' professional networks to surface warm introduction paths and automate referral programs.
enrichment.seo_title: Commsor: Warm Intro & Referral Platform for B2B Sales
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Commsor maps your team's network to find warm intro paths to target accounts and automates referral programs with tracked payouts, from $1,000/month.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Commsor is a B2B go-to-market platform founded in 2019 in New York by Jacob Peters and Mac Reddin. The company originally built "Community OS," a community-led growth platform for tracking and operationalizing branded community engagement, and raised a $50 million round anchored by Atomico with participation from SignalFire, Slack Fund, 776, Felicis, and more than 150 individual angel investors, reportedly reaching a valuation near $450 million at the time, with roughly $68 million raised in total across its history. Since then, Commsor has narrowed its focus and rebranded around a product it calls "Go-to-Network": rather than tracking general community engagement, it maps a sales team's collective professional network — investors, advisors, partners, alumni, and existing customers — to surface warm introduction paths into target accounts, instead of relying on cold outreach. Core capabilities include network mapping to a target account through people a rep already knows, scalable referral-program automation with deal registration and tracked payouts, and signal-based alerts that notify reps when a target account experiences a newsworthy event, such as raising funding, making a senior hire, or posting a relevant job opening, cross-referenced against who on the sales team has an existing connection there. It's important to clarify Commsor's relationship to Common Room, a separate, similarly named company in an adjacent category: the two have never merged or been acquired by one another. They are, and have historically been, competing or adjacent companies — Common Room in AI-native customer/buyer intelligence (and, as of July 2026, in the process of being acquired by Zoom) and Commsor in network-based referral and warm-introduction tooling. Third-party research and comparison sites have sometimes listed the two alongside each other as alternatives in the community/GTM-intelligence space, which may be the source of confusion about a merger, but no such transaction has taken place. Commsor's current pricing has two published tiers: a Software plan at $1,000/month covering 5+ team seats, 50+ data connectors, unlimited personas and playbooks, and CRM integrations with Salesforce and HubSpot, plus a dedicated customer success manager; and a Managed Service plan at $6,500/month that adds a dedicated Go-to-Network assistant who runs campaign execution and hands-off network management on the customer's behalf. A proof-of-concept trial is available before committing to either plan. Commsor is aimed at B2B sales and revenue teams — particularly at venture-backed startups with active investor, advisor, and customer networks — who want to systematically convert "who you know" into pipeline rather than relying purely on outbound cold outreach or paid intent data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Commsor is a go-to-market platform, founded in 2019 in New York by Jacob Peters and Mac Reddin, that has evolved from a general community-led growth ('Community OS') product into a more focused 'Go-to-Network' tool for B2B sales teams.

Rather than tracking generic community engagement, the current product maps a sales organization's collective professional network to find warm paths into target accounts through investors, advisors, partners, and existing customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Commsor's network mapping identifies who on a sales team already has a relationship with people at a target account, replacing cold outreach with warm introduction paths.

Referral program automation includes deal registration and tracked payouts for partners, advisors, or customers who make introductions, and signal-based alerts notify reps when a target account raises funding, hires, or posts a relevant job so they can act on timely, relevant openings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Commsor's Software plan starts at $1,000/month for 5+ seats, 50+ data connectors, and CRM integrations with Salesforce and HubSpot. A Managed Service plan at $6,500/month adds a dedicated assistant who runs referral and network campaigns on the customer's behalf.
A proof-of-concept trial is available before purchase, and pricing above the published tiers is handled through a sales conversation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Company History and Relationship to Common Room
paragraphs: Commsor was originally built as a community-led growth platform and raised a $50 million round in a period when community-led growth was a heavily funded category, reportedly reaching a valuation near $450 million.

Commsor and Common Room are separate companies that have never merged; despite similar-sounding names and overlapping categories, no acquisition between them has occurred. Common Room, unrelated to Commsor, is itself in the process of being acquired by Zoom as of July 2026.

Managed service option available for teams that don't want to run network campaigns themselves
Native CRM integrations (Salesforce, HubSpot) keep reps in their existing workflow
Proof-of-concept trial available before committing to a paid plan
cons: Entry pricing starts at $1,000/month, higher than many self-serve sales tools
Full pricing for larger teams or the managed service tier requires a sales conversation
Value depends heavily on having an active investor/advisor/customer network to map in the first place
The company has pivoted its core product focus over time (from community-led growth to Go-to-Network), which is worth confirming is still current before purchase

faqs.question: Did Commsor merge with Common Room?
faqs.answer: No. Commsor and Common Room are separate companies that have never merged. They have historically operated in adjacent categories and have sometimes been listed as competitors or alternatives, which may explain the confusion, but no acquisition or merger between them has taken place.

faqs.question: What does Commsor do today?
faqs.answer: Commsor currently offers a 'Go-to-Network' product that maps a sales team's professional network to find warm introduction paths to target accounts and automates referral programs, a shift from its earlier community-led growth ('Community OS') positioning.

faqs.question: How much does Commsor cost?
faqs.answer: Commsor's Software plan starts at $1,000/month for 5+ seats and core network-mapping features. A Managed Service plan with a dedicated assistant running campaigns costs $6,500/month.

faqs.question: Who is Commsor for?
faqs.answer: It's built for B2B sales and revenue teams, particularly at venture-backed startups with active investor, advisor, and customer networks they want to convert into warm sales pipeline.

faqs.question: Does Commsor offer a trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, a proof-of-concept trial is available to test the platform before subscribing.

enrichment.short_description: Compa is an AI-driven compensation intelligence platform that gives enterprise comp and recruiting teams live, verified market pay data for offers and employees.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compa gives enterprise comp teams live, verified market pay data and AI agents for offers, equity, and skills-based benchmarking. Used by Apple, NVIDIA.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Compa is an AI-driven compensation intelligence platform for enterprise compensation, total rewards, and recruiting teams, founded in 2020 by Charlie Franklin (CEO), Joe Malandruccolo, and Taylor Cone. The company is based in Irvine, California, with additional offices in Newport Beach and Fountain Valley, California, and has around 50 to 60 employees. Compa has raised roughly $20 million in total funding, including a $10 million Series B round. Its core pitch is replacing traditional, slow, survey-based compensation benchmarking with continuous, live market data: the platform connects directly to a company's HR, HRIS, ATS, and equity systems (integrating with tools like Workday, Oracle, Greenhouse, Carta, and E*TRADE) so that pay data updates automatically rather than requiring teams to manually participate in periodic compensation surveys. Compa's market data spans five categories — Offers, Employees, Stock, Skills, and Frontline — and is built from more than 9 million pay observations across more than 50 countries and industries including technology, healthcare, retail, finance, professional services, and manufacturing. This lets comp teams benchmark not just against a job title, but against specific skills, which the company argues better reflects how compensation actually works in fast-moving fields like AI and life sciences, where roles and skill sets change faster than standardized job codes can keep up. Compa also offers grant-level equity compensation data, letting companies benchmark stock and RSU grants alongside cash compensation. In 2026, Compa's product messaging has shifted toward AI agents: an "Analyst Agent" that works alongside a company's compensation team to analyze market data and produce executive-ready answers to pay questions in seconds, and a "Partner Agent" that lets non-compensation stakeholders in recruiting and HR self-serve pay guidance for thousands of individual hiring or promotion decisions without looping in the core comp team for every request. The platform is SOC 2 Type II certified and compliant with GDPR and CCPA, reflecting its focus on serving large enterprises handling sensitive compensation data. Compa reports serving more than 100 customers, including well-known enterprises such as Apple, NVIDIA, Tesla, Mastercard, T-Mobile, Sanofi, Moderna, and Gilead Sciences, spanning technology and life sciences sectors where compensation decisions are high-stakes and need to reflect real-time market conditions. Pricing is enterprise-oriented and not aimed at small businesses: the Market Data tier starts at $35,000 per year, with multi-year discounts and pilot pricing available on request, while the newer Agents tier is priced through a direct sales conversation. As a relatively small, venture-backed company serving a specialized enterprise HR-tech niche, Compa is best understood as a focused compensation-intelligence vendor rather than a broad HR platform, competing with players like OpenComp, Pave, and Ravio in the compensation benchmarking space.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Compa is an AI-driven compensation intelligence platform founded in 2020 by Charlie Franklin, Joe Malandruccolo, and Taylor Cone, headquartered in Irvine, California. It replaces traditional, periodic compensation surveys with continuous, live market pay data pulled directly from a company's HR, ATS, and equity systems.
The platform is aimed at enterprise compensation, total rewards, and recruiting teams that need to benchmark pay decisions in real time rather than against stale survey data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Compa's market data covers Offers, Employees, Stock, Skills, and Frontline pay categories, drawn from more than 9 million observations across 50+ countries, and supports skills-based benchmarking rather than relying solely on standardized job titles.

Two AI agents extend the core data product: an Analyst Agent that helps compensation teams analyze the market and produce executive-ready answers, and a Partner Agent that lets recruiting and HR stakeholders self-serve pay guidance for individual hiring decisions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Compa's Market Data tier starts at $35,000 per year, with multi-year discounts and pilot pricing available on request. The Agents tier, which adds the Analyst and Partner AI agents, is priced through a direct sales conversation rather than published rate cards.
This positions Compa firmly as an enterprise tool rather than a self-serve product for small teams.

Agents tier pricing is not public and requires a sales conversation
Best value requires deep HRIS/ATS/equity system integration, adding implementation effort
Small vendor (~50-60 employees) relative to larger HR-tech platforms

faqs.answer: Compa is an AI-driven compensation intelligence platform that gives enterprise comp and recruiting teams live, continuously updated market pay data for offers, employees, and equity, instead of relying on periodic manual surveys.

faqs.question: How much does Compa cost?
faqs.answer: The Market Data tier starts at $35,000 per year, with multi-year discounts and pilot pricing available. The Agents tier is custom-priced through sales.

faqs.question: Who uses Compa?
faqs.answer: Compa is used by enterprise compensation, total rewards, and recruiting teams, with reported customers including Apple, NVIDIA, Tesla, Mastercard, T-Mobile, Sanofi, Moderna, and Gilead Sciences.

faqs.answer: Compa integrates with HR, ATS, and equity systems such as Workday, Oracle, Greenhouse, Carta, and E*TRADE to keep pay data continuously synced.

enrichment.short_description: Compose AI is a free Chrome extension that adds AI-powered autocomplete and text generation to any website, helping users write emails and messages faster.
enrichment.seo_title: Compose AI: Free AI Writing Autocomplete Extension
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compose AI is a free Chrome extension that autocompletes your writing anywhere on the web, rephrases text, and drafts email replies using AI.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Compose AI is a browser-based AI writing assistant distributed as a free Chrome extension, backed by Y Combinator. Rather than being a standalone writing application, it embeds directly into the Chrome browser and adds inline AI autocomplete to whatever website or web app a user is typing in, such as Gmail, Google Docs, LinkedIn, or a company's internal support tool. Public sources differ slightly on the exact founding details and founding team, but the product is consistently described as a small, YC-backed startup that has been publicly available since around 2020. Its central feature is autocomplete: a user can type two forward slashes ("//") followed by a short instruction to generate text with AI, or simply keep typing and accept inline suggestions the extension offers based on context, similar to how code editors offer autocomplete. Beyond basic autocomplete, Compose AI includes a rephrasing tool that can adjust a selected passage of text to sound friendlier, more professional, shorter, or longer, and an email-specific feature that can generate a full draft reply from a short phrase or set of bullet points, or expand a short note into a complete, properly formatted email. A personalization layer is designed to learn an individual user's writing voice over time and suggest phrases and completions that increasingly match their own style rather than a generic AI tone. According to the company's own marketing site, Compose AI is used by more than 10,000 teams and roughly 400,000 individual users, and is positioned for professionals who send a high volume of email, sales and support messages, job descriptions, or social posts and want a lightweight way to reduce typing time (the company cites internal claims of cutting writing time by up to 40%) without switching to a separate, full-featured AI writing application. Pricing follows a straightforward freemium model: the core autocomplete and rephrasing functionality is free to use indefinitely with no credit card required, while a premium tier unlocks expanded personalization and additional advanced writing features; Compose AI's public marketing pages do not disclose the exact price of the premium tier. Because it is a Chrome extension rather than a standalone product, Compose AI's scope is intentionally narrow compared to full AI writing platforms like Grammarly, Jasper, or Copy.ai — it does not offer long-form content generation, grammar/style scoring, or SEO tooling, and it is currently limited to the Chrome browser rather than being available as a native desktop, iOS, or Android application. It's best understood as a focused productivity add-on for people who already spend most of their workday typing inside a browser and want faster completions without adopting a heavier AI writing suite.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Compose AI is a free, Y Combinator-backed Chrome extension that adds AI-powered autocomplete to any website. Instead of a standalone editor, it works inline wherever a user types — in Gmail, Google Docs, LinkedIn, or internal web tools.
The product has been publicly available since around 2020 and reports being used by more than 10,000 teams and roughly 400,000 individual users.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core feature is inline autocomplete, triggered either by typing "//" followed by an instruction, or simply by accepting suggested completions as you type. A rephrasing tool can adjust selected text to sound friendlier, more professional, shorter, or longer.
An email-specific mode can generate a full draft reply from a short phrase, and a personalization layer learns an individual's writing style over time to make suggestions feel less generic.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Compose AI is free to use indefinitely for its core autocomplete and rephrasing features, with no credit card required. A premium tier adds expanded personalization and additional advanced features; the exact premium price is not disclosed on the public marketing site.

Works across virtually any website rather than being locked to one app
Lightweight and fast compared to switching to a full separate AI writing tool
Backed by Y Combinator with a large reported user base (400,000+ users)
cons: Limited to the Chrome browser, with no native desktop, iOS, or Android app
Narrower feature set than full AI writing platforms (no long-form generation, grammar scoring, or SEO tools)
Premium tier pricing is not publicly disclosed
Company size and update cadence are not transparently published, making long-term support harder to verify

faqs.question: Is Compose AI free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core autocomplete and rephrasing features are free to use indefinitely. A premium tier adds expanded personalization, though its price is not publicly listed.

faqs.question: What browsers does Compose AI support?
faqs.answer: Compose AI is distributed as a Chrome extension via the Chrome Web Store; it is not currently available as a native desktop or mobile app.

faqs.question: How do you trigger Compose AI?
faqs.answer: Type "//" followed by an instruction to generate text with AI, or simply keep typing and accept the inline suggestions it offers.

faqs.question: Who is Compose AI for?
faqs.answer: It's designed for professionals who type a high volume of email, messages, or web-based content and want faster, lighter-weight autocomplete than a full AI writing suite.

enrichment.short_description: Concord is a contract lifecycle management platform combining drafting, real-time collaboration, AI-assisted review, and e-signature in one system.
enrichment.headquarters: Paris, France (with a U.S. office in Austin, Texas)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Concord is a CLM platform for contract drafting, AI-assisted review, e-signature, and approval workflows, used by 1,500+ SMB and mid-market companies.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Concord (also known as Concord Worldwide, or Concord CLM) is a contract lifecycle management (CLM) platform founded in 2014, reportedly by CEO Matt Lhoumeau together with Florian Parain, and headquartered in Paris, France, with a U.S. office in Austin, Texas. The company has raised approximately $38.7 million in funding from investors including CRV, Tenaya Capital, and Alven, and reports serving more than 1,500 small-to-mid-market organizations, generally in the 50-to-1,000-employee range, across North America and Europe. Concord's positioning is that it combines the full contract lifecycle into a single system rather than requiring teams to stitch together a separate word processor, e-signature tool, and spreadsheet-based contract tracker. Core functionality includes contract drafting in a Word- or Google-Docs-like editor, real-time internal and external collaboration on drafts, legally binding e-signature with unlimited free viewer and guest access, and automated deadline, renewal, and amendment reminders so contracts don't silently lapse. An AI Copilot feature assists with contract review and data extraction, helping legal and procurement teams quickly identify key clauses, obligations, and risk points across a document without manual line-by-line review. Concord's pricing is structured in three tiers, each billed annually and including five users, with additional users billed per seat per month. The Essentials plan is $499/month (additional users at $49/month each) and includes the AI Copilot, unlimited e-signatures and documents, collaboration tools, deadline reminders, and amendment management. The Business plan is $899/month (additional users at $69/month each) and adds intake forms, approval workflows, custom roles and teams, customized reporting, and integrations with Salesforce, HubSpot, Google Drive, Box, Dropbox, and Zapier, plus a Slack integration in beta. The Enterprise plan is $1,299/month (additional users at $89/month each) and adds a clause library, subsidiary and multi-entity contract management, bulk send capability, an open API, and custom branding, with volume discounts available on request. All plans include bank-level encryption and unlimited free external viewers/guests, meaning counterparties don't need a paid seat to review or sign a contract. A 14-day free trial with full feature access is available before committing to a paid plan, and there are no disclosed setup or onboarding fees. Concord competes in the broader CLM market alongside tools like Ironclad, DocuSign CLM, PandaDoc, and Juro, and it is generally positioned toward SMB and mid-market legal, procurement, sales, and HR teams that want an all-in-one contract system rather than an enterprise-only CLM built primarily for large legal departments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Concord is a contract lifecycle management platform founded in 2014 and headquartered in Paris, France, with a U.S. office in Austin, Texas. It combines contract drafting, collaboration, e-signature, and post-signature management into a single system.

The company reports serving more than 1,500 SMB and mid-market organizations across North America and Europe, targeting legal, procurement, sales, and HR teams that want to manage the full contract lifecycle in one tool rather than separate point solutions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Contracts can be drafted directly in Concord's Word- and Google-Docs-like editor, with real-time collaboration for both internal stakeholders and external counterparties, and legally binding e-signature built in.

An AI Copilot assists with contract review and data extraction, surfacing key clauses and obligations, while automated deadline, renewal, and amendment reminders help prevent contracts from silently lapsing.
Higher tiers add approval workflows, custom intake forms, a clause library, and integrations with Salesforce, HubSpot, Google Drive, Box, Dropbox, and Zapier.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Concord offers three annually billed tiers, each including 5 users: Essentials at $499/month, Business at $899/month, and Enterprise at $1,299/month, with additional users billed per seat. All plans include unlimited free external viewers and guests, so counterparties never need a paid seat to sign.
A 14-day free trial with full feature access is available, and there are no disclosed setup or onboarding fees.

Unlimited free external viewers and guests means counterparties never need a paid account
Flat per-tier pricing with no per-document or per-signature fees
14-day free trial with full feature access before purchase
cons: Entry pricing starts at $499/month, higher than lightweight e-signature-only tools
Advanced workflow and clause-library features are reserved for Business and Enterprise tiers
Slack integration is still in beta
Best suited to SMB/mid-market teams; very large enterprises with complex legal ops may need deeper customization than the platform offers

faqs.question: How much does Concord cost?
faqs.answer: Concord's Essentials plan starts at $499/month billed annually for 5 users, with Business at $899/month and Enterprise at $1,299/month, each adding more workflow and integration features.

faqs.question: Does Concord offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Concord offers a 14-day free trial with full access to paid features before requiring a subscription.

faqs.question: Do external parties need to pay to sign a contract in Concord?
faqs.answer: No, all Concord plans include unlimited free viewers and guests, so counterparties can review and sign without a paid seat.

faqs.question: Does Concord have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, an open API is available on the Enterprise plan for custom integrations.

faqs.question: What does Concord's AI Copilot do?
faqs.answer: It assists with contract review and data extraction, helping teams identify key clauses, obligations, and risk points without manual line-by-line review.

features: AI Copilot for review and extraction
Salesforce, HubSpot, Google Drive, Box, Dropbox, Zapier integrations
